primary_diagnosis.name: Major thermal and chemical burn — approximately 35% TBSA mixed partial- and full-thickness, with suspected inhalation injury and impending hypovolaemic (burn) shock

comorbidities.name: Hypothermia from loss of thermoregulatory skin barrier

pathophysiology_summary: A flash fire plus boiling-oil contact produced an extensive mixed-depth cutaneous burn (approximately 35% TBSA) over the anterior torso, bilateral upper limbs and face. Full-thickness zones destroy dermal nociceptors, producing the characteristic painless, leathery, charred eschar — absence of pain is a depth marker, not reassurance. Loss of the epidermal barrier drives massive evaporative fluid and heat loss; combined with mediator-driven capillary leak this causes burn (distributive/hypovolaemic) shock and hypothermia. Concurrent thermal and products-of-combustion exposure in a confined space causes an inhalation injury: mucosal oedema and progressive upper-airway swelling that can occlude the airway within minutes (hoarse voice, singed facial hair, carbonaceous sputum, stridor are the warning signs), plus a risk of carbon monoxide toxicity. The airway is the immediate threat to life; circulatory collapse and hypothermia are close behind. Definitive care is high-flow oxygen, early airway control (often intubation before oedema seals the airway), formal burns fluid resuscitation and transfer to a specialist burns unit — none of which is deliverable by a telehealth service; the telehealth-correct action is immediate 000 activation and carer-level first aid.

differential.diagnosis: Major thermal/chemical burn with airway and circulatory compromise
differential.evidence_basis: Witnessed flash fire and boiling-oil injury with extensive burns, hoarse voice and breathing difficulty within minutes.
Considered but does not fit charred painless eschar, the large surface area, or the haemodynamic and airway involvement.

differential.diagnosis: Major burn (~35% TBSA) with inhalation injury and burn shock
differential.evidence_basis: Charred painless full-thickness eschar, hoarse voice/singed facial hair (airway), and dizziness/cold/pallor (shock) together define a major-burn emergency.
Charred painless eschar, 35% TBSA and haemodynamic instability exclude a minor scald.
The airway swelling and shock are fully explained by a thermal/chemical explosion mechanism; there is no allergen exposure.
Chest tightness and hypotension are explained by inhalation injury and burn-related fluid loss, not primary cardiac ischaemia.

differential.diagnosis: Major thermal/chemical burn ~35% TBSA with inhalation injury and impending hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Definitive on-scene assessment; time-critical airway and shock — mandatory emergency transport to a burns unit.

red_flags.description: Hoarse voice, singed facial hair, throat burning and noisy breathing after a fire in a confined space — impending inhalation-injury airway obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Airway oedema can progress to complete occlusion within minutes; this is the immediate threat to life and mandates emergency airway backup.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal airway obstruction before help arrives if not escalated immediately.

red_flags.description: Painless, charred, leathery eschar over the chest — full-thickness burn depth
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of pain signals destruction of the full dermis; a severity marker requiring specialist burns care, not a reason for reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Underestimating burn depth and severity, leading to under-triage.

red_flags.description: Dizziness, feeling cold, pallor and sweating — burn shock and hypothermia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distributive/hypovolaemic shock with heat loss; time-critical fluid resuscitation and warming are required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible shock and death.

red_flags.description: Extensive burn surface area (~35% TBSA) across torso, both arms and face
red_flags.clinical_significance: A burn of this size is a major-burn emergency requiring a specialist burns unit regardless of depth.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Interpreting the absence of pain over the charred chest as a good sign, when it actually signals full-thickness depth and greater severity.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Cool-the-burn reflex in a major burn
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Applying the standard 'cool running water / ice' first-aid advice to a >20% TBSA burn with developing hypothermia — this deepens hypothermia and can worsen shock. The correct carer action here is to keep the patient warm.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the chest tightness as cardiac
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating chest tightness and hypotension as a primary cardiac event and missing that they are burn/inhalation driven.
Being falsely reassured because the patient is still speaking; inhalation-injury airway oedema can occlude rapidly, so a currently-patent airway does not lower the tier.
Attempting to talk the spouse through wound care or fluids instead of escalating; definitive burn care (airway control, oxygen, IV resuscitation, burns unit) cannot be delivered remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Accurate blood pressure and heart rate (measured on scene BP 85/50, HR 145)
Respiratory rate and oxygen saturation (measured RR 32, SpO2 91% room air)
Formal TBSA estimation and burn-depth mapping (35% TBSA, full-thickness eschar)
Confirmation of analgesia in the full-thickness zones (neurological exam)
Carbonaceous sputum in the oropharynx and auscultated inspiratory stridor
Peripheral pulse volume and perfusion assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 for an emergency ambulance for a major burn with airway threat and shock; keep the patient warm and still, do NOT apply water/ice/creams to the burns, do not attempt private transport, unlock the door for paramedics, and be ready to describe deterioration. Definitive oxygen, airway control and IV fluid resuscitation are paramedic/ED tasks.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a major-burn emergency (mechanism and findings are unambiguous). The real risk is UNDER-action — under-triaging because the patient is still talking, or giving harmful 'cool the burn' first aid that deepens hypothermia.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Airway assessment first in suspected inhalation injury; early intubation before oedema occludes the airway; formal fluid resuscitation for burns >20% TBSA; prevent hypothermia; refer major burns to a specialist burns unit.
For extensive burns, prioritise calling an ambulance and preventing hypothermia; cooling is not appropriate when it would cause dangerous heat loss.
